terms.definition: Python's built-in immutable sequence of Unicode code points used to represent text.
Python's `bytes` type — an immutable sequence of 8-bit integers, used at I/O boundaries (network, files, hashes).
An integer that identifies a character in the Unicode standard; what `len(str)` counts.
A variable-length byte encoding of Unicode (1–4 bytes per code point); the default for `str.encode()`/`bytes.decode()`.
An f-prefixed string literal that interpolates Python expressions and supports format specs, e.g. `f"${cost:.2f}"`.
A model-specific algorithm (BPE, SentencePiece) that splits text into the units an LLM actually consumes; a character is not a token.
Cannot be modified in place; every 'modification' produces a new object — true of `str`, `bytes` and `tuple`.
